title: Litigation-hold override audit — holds applied/released without ticket
exclusions: Does NOT assess legal sufficiency; that is a human review.
evidence: Hourly 'litigation_hold_audit' writes unapproved hold changes to compliance_summary.

compliance.assurance_rationale: SOX and e-discovery obligations require documented litigation-hold processes; this UC detects untracked hold changes.
compliance.controlObjective: Evidence that SOX ITGC ITGC.Logging.Review (Log review) is enforced — Splunk UC-22.49.3: Litigation-hold override audit — holds applied/released without ticket.
compliance.evidenceArtifact: Saved search 'UC-22.49.3' running on catalogue-defined data sources (see UC detailedImplementation), archived to the restricted audit_evidence index (default 7-year retention). Auto-drafted — SME review required.

compliance.assurance_rationale: A.5.33 protection of records — legal holds are a specific implementation.
compliance.controlObjective: Evidence that ISO 27001 A.5.33 is enforced — Splunk UC-22.49.3: Litigation-hold override audit — holds applied/released without ticket.
compliance.evidenceArtifact: Saved search 'UC-22.49.3' running on catalogue-defined data sources (see UC detailedImplementation), archived to the restricted audit_evidence index (default 7-year retention). Auto-drafted — SME review required.

controlTest.positiveScenario: When a litigation-hold is applied or released on any in-scope record and no matching legal ticket exists within 24 hours, the UC fires.
controlTest.negativeScenario: Hold changes accompanied by a valid legal ticket do NOT fire.

spl:
index=datastore sourcetype=hold:change earliest=-24h
| join hold_id [search index=legal sourcetype=hold:ticket | fields hold_id ticket_id requested_by]
| where isnull(ticket_id)
| table _time hold_id record_id action ticket_id
description: Detects unauthorised application or release of litigation holds — both of which are common sources of adverse inference in discovery.
value: Prevents silent tampering with evidence preservation.
implementation: (1) Ingest hold-change audit events from data stores and from the legal-hold tool; (2) run hourly. Indexes required: `index=datastore`, `index=legal`; Sourcetypes: `sourcetype=hold:change`, `sourcetype=hold:ticket`.
visualization: Table of unauthorised changes, single-value 'hold changes with ticket (30d)'. Key fields: `action`, `hold_id`, `record_id`, `ticket_id`.
knownFalsePositives: Bulk hold changes during DR or system migration should carry a maintenance ticket.
grandmaExplanation: We check on detects unauthorised application or release of litigation holds — both of which are common sources of adverse inference in discovery. This matters because it catches problems that could lead to audit findings or compliance gaps.
detailedImplementation:
Prerequisites
• Install and configure the required add-on or app: Splunk Enterprise / Splunk Cloud Platform.
• Ensure the following data sources are available: See subcategory preamble..
• For app installation, inputs.conf, and Splunk directory layout, see the Implementation guide: docs/implementation-guide.md

Step 1 — Configure data collection
(1) Ingest hold-change audit events from data stores and from the legal-hold tool; (2) run hourly.

Step 2 — Create the search and alert
Run the following SPL in Search (then save as report or alert; adjust time range and threshold as needed):

```spl
index=datastore sourcetype=hold:change earliest=-24h
| join hold_id [search index=legal sourcetype=hold:ticket | fields hold_id ticket_id requested_by]
| where isnull(ticket_id)
| table _time hold_id record_id action ticket_id
```

Understanding this SPL

**Litigation-hold override audit — holds applied/released without ticket** — Prevents silent tampering with evidence preservation.

Documented **Data sources**: See subcategory preamble. **App/TA** (typical add-on context): Splunk Enterprise / Splunk Cloud Platform. The SPL below should target the same indexes and sourcetypes you configured for that feed—rename `index=` / `sourcetype=` if your deployment differs.

The first pipeline stage scopes events using **index**: datastore; **sourcetype**: hold:change. If that sourcetype is not mentioned in Data sources, double-check parsing or update the documentation to match the feed you actually ingest.

**Pipeline walkthrough**

• Scopes the data: index=datastore, sourcetype=hold:change, time bounds. Cross-check against **Data sources** above so indexes and sourcetypes match your ingestion.
• Correlates data from a subsearch to enrich the primary result set with additional context.
• Filters the current rows with `where isnull(ticket_id)` — typically the threshold or rule expression for this monitoring goal.

Output fields: _time, action, hold_id, record_id, ticket_id.

Step 3 — Validate
Confirm that events are present in the index and that the search returns expected results. Compare with known good/bad scenarios if applicable. Verify field extractions and index permissions.

Step 4 — Operationalize
Add the search to a dashboard or set up alert actions (email, webhook, PagerDuty, etc.) as required. Document the use case in your runbook and assign an owner. Consider visualizations: Table of unauthorised changes, single-value 'hold changes with ticket (30d)'.
